questions.question: Explain the divide‑and‑conquer strategy employed in Merge Sort and state its time complexity.
keyPoints: Divide: split the array into two roughly equal halves
Combine: merge the two sorted halves into one sorted array
Time complexity O(n log n) in worst, average and best cases

questions.question: Describe the basic steps of Quick Sort and the role of the pivot element.
keyPoints: Pick a pivot element from the array
Partition: rearrange so elements less than pivot are on the left, greater on the right
Recursively apply Quick Sort to the left and right sub‑arrays
Average‑case time O(n log n); worst‑case O(n^2) when pivot is poorly chosen

questions.question: Give the pseudocode for Merge Sort and illustrate the merge operation with a concrete example of an 8‑element array. Also discuss its space complexity.
keyPoints: Pseudocode: mergeSort(arr, l, r) recursively splits, merge(arr, l, m, r) merges
Merge step: create auxiliary array, compare elements from two halves and copy back in order
Example: 38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10 → sorted order after successive merges
Space complexity O(n) due to the temporary array required for merging

questions.question: Write the pseudocode for Quick Sort using the Lomuto partition scheme. Explain how the choice of pivot influences performance, and compare Quick Sort with Merge Sort regarding in‑place nature, stability, and typical runtime.
keyPoints: Pseudocode: quickSort(arr, low, high) calls partition(arr, low, high) then recurses
Lomuto partition: pivot = arr[high], maintain index i for smaller elements, swap arr[i] and arr[j]
Pivot selection (first, last, median, random) affects worst‑case; poor choices lead to O(n^2)
Comparison: Quick Sort is in‑place (O(log n) stack), not stable; Merge Sort is stable, uses O(n) extra space, guarantees O(n log n)
